title: What a blower door test tells you about your home
subtitle: Understanding air permeability results and what to fix based on the number
excerpt: A blower door test (also called a pressurisation test) measures how much air leaks through your building envelope under a standard 50 Pa pressure difference. The result is usually expressed as air changes per hour at 50 Pa, written q50 or ACH50. Most UK existing homes measure 10-15 ACH50; a good retrofit target is below 5.
sourceNotes: ATTMA TS1 (Air Tightness Testing and Measurement Association Technical Standard 1) for test methodology and result interpretation. Gov.uk Part L1B Approved Document for compliance targets. Passivhaus Institut criteria (0.6 ACH50 for full certification). BRE Good Practice Guide for existing homes airtightness.

content.text: A blower door test fits a calibrated fan into an external doorway and depressurises the building to a standard 50 pascals (Pa). The fan measures the flow rate in m3/hour needed to maintain that pressure difference. Divide by the building volume and you get ACH50: air changes per hour at 50 Pa. A UK 1930s semi-detached with no airtightness work typically measures 12-18 ACH50. A careful retrofit with well-sealed wall insulation and draught-proofing can reach 3-5 ACH50. Passivhaus standard is 0.6 ACH50.
What a typical result means for heating cost

A rough rule of thumb for UK existing homes: each 1 ACH50 reduction saves approximately 120-200 kWh/year in a typical semi-detached (120 m3 volume, gas heated, 35% of heating days with significant wind). Reducing from 14 to 6 ACH50 saves 960-1,600 kWh/year, worth a modest cost at 6p/kWh gas (2025/26 cap rates). These are indicative; actual savings depend on building volume, wind exposure, and whether the building has extract ventilation.
During the test, with the fan maintaining depressurisation, you can walk the house and feel for leaks with the back of your hand. Common locations in older UK housing:
Loft hatch perimeter (often accounts for 5-10% of total infiltration in an unsealed house)
Chimney breast and redundant flues (a blocked chimney with no balloon can account for 20% of infiltration)
Suspended floor perimeter. Service penetrations through the floor, especially around pipes entering from below, are a common air path.
External wall sockets and switches in older houses where the back box connects to the cavity
Window and door frames, particularly at corners and where the frame meets masonry
Soil-pipe and gas-pipe entry points through the external wall

A tight building needs controlled ventilation. At 3-5 ACH50, natural infiltration is no longer enough to maintain indoor air quality in a house with normal occupancy, cooking, and bathing. Tightening below 5 ACH50 without adding extract ventilation (at minimum, trickle vents on windows plus bathroom/kitchen extraction) creates moisture build-up and elevated CO2 levels. If you are targeting below 5 ACH50 as part of a retrofit, plan ventilation before you seal.

ATTMA-registered testers advertise regionally. A test takes 2-4 hours and results in a formal certificate. Some energy companies include testing in a retrofit package. You can hire equipment for self-testing, though reading the result accurately needs familiarity with the ATTMA TS1 standard.
